content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

SUPERSEDEL
byReg.18533

January 3, 1986

Paul McCauley, C.P.A.
11726 San Vicente Blvd.
Suite 350
Los Angeles, CA 90049

Re: FPPC NO. A-85-243

Dear Mr. McCauley:

This is in reply to your letter dated October 24, 1985,
which was received in this office on November 25, 1985. I
apologize for the delay in responding to your letter which,
unfortunately, was misplaced in our office.

Because the question posed in your letter is not asked on
behalf of a specifically identified person, this letter does not
constitute advice under Gov. Code Section 83114. (See 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18239(b), copy enclosed.) Rather, this letter
provides a general interpretation of the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act.

You have asked the following question:

A contributor and his spouse have contributed in
excess of $10,000 but less than $20,000 to political
candidates and committees during the calendar year out
of their community funds. Is either the husband or
wife liable for filing a major donor political report?

Contributions from community funds must be aggregated for
the purpose of qualifying as a major donor committee under Gov.
Code Section 82013(c). Community funds are a single source of
funds, and it does not matter whether the contributions are
actually directed by one spouse or the other.

Therefore, once contributions totalling $10,000 or more in a
calendar year have been made from community funds, the husband and
wife have qualified as a committee and must file campaign
disclosure statements.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Paul McCauley
January 3, 1986
Page 2




Enclosed for your information are a copy of the 1985 "Information
Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform
Act," and an "Expenditure and Major Donor Committee Campaign
Statement" (Form 461).

     Please call our Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
at (916) 322-5662 if you would like specific information regarding
the filing of campaign statements by a major donor committee.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Jeanne Pritchard
                                        Chief, Technical Assistance
                                        & Analysis Division

JP:kt

Enclosure

PAUL McCAULEY, C.P.A.
11726 SAN VICENTE BOULEVARD - SUITE 350
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90049
(213) 207-2069 Nov 23 2 50 PM 05

October 24, 1985


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Gentlemen:

Clarification is requested concerning the following circumstance:

     A contributor and his spouse have contributed in excess
of $10,000 but less than $20,000 to political candidates
and committees during the calendar year out of their
community funds. Is either husband or wife liable for
filing a Major donor political report?

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Paul McCauley, C.P.A.

sections.question: A contributor and his spouse have contributed in excess of $10,000 but less than $20,000 to political candidates and committees during the calendar year out of their community funds. Is either the husband or wife liable for filing a major donor political report?
sections.conclusion: Contributions from community funds must be aggregated for the purpose of qualifying as a major donor committee under Gov. Code section 82013(c). Community funds are a single source of funds, and it does not matter whether the contributions are actually directed by one spouse or the other. Therefore, once contributions totalling $10,000 or more in a calendar year have been made from community funds, the husband and wife have qualified as a committee and must file campaign disclosure statements.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a married couple must file a major donor political report when they have made contributions exceeding $10,000 in a calendar year from their community funds.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that contributions from community funds must be aggregated, and since the couple contributed over $10,000 combined, both the husband and wife must file campaign disclosure statements as they have qualified as a committee.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Letter dated January 3, 1986 responding to October 24, 1985 inquiry (FPPC No. A-85-243). FPPC notes that because question was not asked on behalf of a specifically identified person, the letter provides general interpretation rather than formal advice under Gov. Code Section 83114. All standard sections (question, conclusion) present and clearly identified.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A contributor and his spouse have contributed in excess of $10,000 but less than $20,000 to political candidates and committees during the calendar year out of their community funds. Is either the husband or wife liable for filing a major donor political report?

CONCLUSION: Contributions from community funds must be aggregated for the purpose of qualifying as a major donor committee under Gov. Code section 82013(c). Community funds are a single source of funds, and it does not matter whether the contributions are actually directed by one spouse or the other. Therefore, once contributions totalling $10,000 or more in a calendar year have been made from community funds, the husband and wife have qualified as a committee and must file campaign disclosure statements.

Is either husband or wife liable for filing a Major donor political report? Very truly yours, [signature] Paul McCauley, C.P.A.
embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that a married couple who contributed between $10,000 and $20,000 from community funds to political candidates and committees must file major donor campaign disclosure statements because community funds are treated as a single source for aggregation purposes.
